subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Intermolecular forces govern the physical properties of substances, and gas behavior can be predicted from the ideal gas law and kinetic molecular theory.
core_concepts: IMF strength order: ion-dipole > hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion forces (LDF); stronger IMF = higher boiling point, melting point, viscosity, surface tension.
LDF (dispersion forces) exist in ALL substances; increase with molar mass and surface area (branched molecules have lower boiling points than straight-chain isomers).
Hydrogen bonding requires H bonded to F, O, or N; responsible for water's unusually high boiling point, density anomaly, and surface tension.
The ideal gas law: $PV = nRT$ assumes no IMF and negligible particle volume; real gases deviate most at high pressure and low temperature.
Kinetic molecular theory: average KE = $\frac{3}{2}RT$; lighter gases move faster at the same temperature (Graham's law: $r \propto 1/\sqrt{M}$).
Phase diagrams map solid, liquid, gas regions; the triple point is where all three coexist; the critical point is the end of the liquid-gas boundary.
Solid types: metallic (delocalized electrons), ionic (electrostatic), covalent network (covalent bonds), molecular (IMF); properties differ dramatically by type.

key_terms.definition: Temporary dipole–induced dipole forces present in all molecules; only IMF in nonpolar molecules
Strong dipole-dipole interaction between H (bonded to F, O, or N) and lone pair on another F, O, or N
Pressure of a vapor above its liquid at equilibrium; increases with temperature; inversely related to IMF strength
Temperature and pressure at which solid, liquid, and gas phases coexist in equilibrium
Temperature and pressure above which liquid and gas phases are indistinguishable (supercritical fluid)
Escape of gas molecules through a small opening; rate $\propto 1/\sqrt{M}$ (Graham's law)
Modified ideal gas law accounting for particle volume ($b$) and intermolecular attractions ($a$)
$u_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{3RT/M}$; average speed of gas molecules; increases with temperature, decreases with molar mass

exam_tip: Unit 3 accounts for 18–22% of the AP Chemistry exam — expect questions on ranking boiling points using IMFs, interpreting phase diagrams, and applying the ideal gas law with non-standard conditions.
